title: Defense in Depth: Azure Firewall, WAF & DDoS Protection
summary: NSGs are the per-VM door locks; this lesson is the building's security system. Azure Firewall controls and inspects traffic in/out of your network, a Web Application Firewall blocks web attacks like SQL injection, and DDoS Protection absorbs volumetric floods. Three different layers for three different threats.
analogy: Think of a castle: DDoS Protection is the moat absorbing the charging horde; Azure Firewall is the gatehouse deciding who may enter or leave and watching for smugglers; the WAF is the inspector at the throne-room door who frisks each visitor for hidden weapons (malicious requests).
diagram:
flowchart TD
  Attack((Internet traffic)) --> DDoS[DDoS Protection: absorb volumetric floods L3/L4]
  DDoS --> Edge{Traffic type?}
  Edge -->|Web app HTTP/S| WAF[WAF on App Gateway/Front Door: block OWASP attacks L7]
  Edge -->|All network traffic| FW[Azure Firewall: central in/out policy, FQDN, threat intel]
  WAF --> App[Web app]
  FW --> Workloads[VNet workloads]
body:
## First principles

No single wall stops every attack, because attacks come in different shapes. A flood of junk traffic trying to exhaust your capacity is nothing like a crafted HTTP request trying to trick your database, which is nothing like malware on a VM phoning home to a command server. **Defense in depth** means layering controls so each handles the threat it's built for — and so a failure in one layer isn't game over. Azure gives you three network-layer defenses that sit at different points and operate at different OSI layers. The skill is knowing which threat each one stops.

## Why it exists — and where NSGs stop

You already met **NSGs**: stateful allow/deny by IP, port, and protocol, attached to subnets and NICs. They're essential but limited — they're **L3/L4 only**. An NSG can say "allow 443 from the internet," but it cannot tell a legitimate HTTPS request from a SQL-injection payload riding inside that 443 traffic, cannot block outbound connections by **domain name**, and cannot absorb a 200 Gbps flood. Each of those gaps is a different product:

- **Volumetric floods (L3/L4) → Azure DDoS Protection.**
- **Malicious web requests (L7/HTTP) → Web Application Firewall (WAF).**
- **Central network traffic control & inspection, especially outbound by FQDN → Azure Firewall.**

## How it works

**Azure DDoS Protection (the moat).** A Distributed Denial-of-Service attack tries to overwhelm you with sheer traffic volume. Azure's platform already has always-on basic DDoS defense for everyone. The paid tiers add tuned, per-resource mitigation, telemetry, alerting, and cost-protection. As of 2025 there are two SKUs: **DDoS Network Protection** (covers all public IPs across protected VNets, plus rapid-response support and cost protection) and **DDoS IP Protection** (a cheaper per-public-IP model for smaller footprints). It operates at **L3/L4** — it scrubs the flood before it reaches you.

**Web Application Firewall (the inspector).** A WAF understands **HTTP** (L7) and inspects the *content* of web requests, blocking the classic application attacks — SQL injection, cross-site scripting, and the rest of the **OWASP Top 10** — using managed rule sets (the OWASP Core Rule Set) plus your custom rules. A WAF is not standalone: it attaches to the **L7 load balancers** — **Application Gateway** (regional) or **Azure Front Door** (global edge). Put it in front of any internet-facing web app.

**Azure Firewall (the gatehouse).** A managed, stateful, scalable network firewall for your whole VNet, typically placed in the **hub** of a hub-and-spoke. Unlike an NSG it does **application-layer** tricks: **FQDN filtering** (allow outbound to `*.ubuntu.com` but nothing else), **network and application rule collections**, **threat-intelligence-based filtering** (auto-block known-malicious IPs/domains), and full logging of every flow. Its superpower is **egress control** — governing and inspecting what leaves your network, which NSGs can't do by name. Premium tier adds TLS inspection and IDPS (intrusion detection/prevention).

**They are not either/or — they stack.** A hardened internet-facing app uses *all* of them: DDoS Protection absorbs floods, Front Door/App Gateway + WAF inspects inbound web requests, Azure Firewall governs outbound and east-west traffic, and NSGs lock down each subnet/NIC. Each layer is cheap insurance against the threat the others can't see.

## In practice

```bash
# Azure Firewall in the hub (needs a subnet named AzureFirewallSubnet).
az network firewall create \
  --name <fw> --resource-group <rg> --location <region>

# Allow outbound only to specific FQDNs - NSGs can't do this.
az network firewall application-rule create \
  --firewall-name <fw> --resource-group <rg> \
  --collection-name allow-updates --name ubuntu \
  --protocols Http=80 Https=443 \
  --target-fqdns '*.ubuntu.com' --source-addresses 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --action Allow --priority 100
```

For the web tier you'd enable a **WAF policy** on your Application Gateway or Front Door, and enable **DDoS Network Protection** on the VNet hosting your public IPs.

## Gotchas

- **Match the layer to the threat.** WAF = HTTP request content (L7). Firewall = network/FQDN/egress control. DDoS = volumetric floods (L3/L4). NSG = basic per-resource IP/port. Using the wrong one leaves a real gap.
- **WAF only protects HTTP(S), and only on L7 front ends.** It rides Application Gateway (regional) or Front Door (global). It does nothing for non-web (e.g. raw TCP) traffic.
- **Azure Firewall needs `AzureFirewallSubnet`.** Like the gateway, it requires a specifically-named subnet; deploy it in the hub so all spokes route egress through it (via UDRs).
- **NSGs don't do FQDNs or payloads.** If you need "allow outbound only to these domains" or content inspection, that's Azure Firewall / WAF territory, not NSGs.
- **Basic DDoS is automatic; tuned protection is a paid SKU.** Don't assume you have full DDoS mitigation — Network Protection vs IP Protection is a deliberate choice with cost implications.
- **Layers complement, they don't replace.** A WAF doesn't remove the need for a firewall or NSGs; defense in depth means keeping all the locks, not picking one.
teachItBack: Different attacks need different walls, so Azure layers network defenses. NSGs give basic per-resource IP/port filtering but are blind to floods, domains, and request content. DDoS Protection absorbs volumetric L3/L4 floods (with Network Protection covering whole VNets and IP Protection a cheaper per-IP option). A Web Application Firewall inspects HTTP content to block OWASP attacks like SQL injection and rides on Application Gateway or Front Door. Azure Firewall is the central gatehouse — usually in the hub — that controls and inspects traffic by FQDN and threat intelligence, especially outbound. The point is defense in depth: a hardened app runs all of them together, each catching what the others can't.

glossary.definition: Layering multiple independent security controls so each covers a different threat and no single failure is fatal.
A managed, stateful, cloud-scale network firewall (usually in the hub) offering FQDN filtering, threat-intel filtering, and centralized in/out traffic control.
An L7 filter that inspects HTTP request content to block web attacks (OWASP Top 10), attached to Application Gateway or Front Door.
Mitigation for volumetric L3/L4 floods; Network Protection covers VNets while IP Protection is a per-public-IP tier.
Allowing or denying traffic by fully-qualified domain name (e.g. *.ubuntu.com) — something NSGs cannot do.
A widely used list of the most critical web application security risks that WAF rule sets are designed to block.
